caveats: a: The occupation source covers all dairy product manufacturing rather than this six-digit industry.
a: Employment counts require judgmental task and wage mapping.
a: The estimate excludes substitutions that require new physical automation rather than AI-enabled workflows.

rho: Modern high-throughput dryers may implement faster than small plants with legacy controls.

e: Industry definitions and survey universes do not identify which enterprises are standalone, recurring external suppliers.

s5: Owner age is not a transaction measure and the source counts responding owners rather than firms.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Dry, Condensed, and Evaporated Dairy Product Manufacturing

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat ingredient or packaged-product supply, commonly priced by weight or unit under contracts, purchase orders, commodity formulas, specifications, or private-label arrangements.
lens.excluded_activities: Fluid milk, creamery butter, cheese, frozen dairy products, captive internal facilities without external customers, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market operators that repeatedly manufacture dry, condensed, evaporated, concentrated, whey, or related dairy and dairy-substitute products for external food, beverage, nutrition, foodservice, distributor, or export customers.

a.bridge: BLS reports occupations for the broader NAICS 311500 dairy-manufacturing group, not wage-weighted current task exposure in 311514. The estimate assigns higher AI exposure to forecasting, scheduling, specification and certificate review, procurement, quality-data triage, maintenance diagnosis, sales support, and administration, and lower exposure to hands-on batching, drying, evaporation, packaging, material movement, maintenance, and driving. Capital-intensive powder plants may have more existing control automation than the broader group, which would overstate remaining exposure; export documentation and ingredient-specification complexity may pull the other way.
caveats: The occupation source covers all dairy product manufacturing rather than this six-digit industry.
Employment counts require judgmental task and wage mapping.
The estimate excludes substitutions that require new physical automation rather than AI-enabled workflows.
a.change_evidence: A 311514 payroll-by-SOC extract and wage-weighted task inventory for planning, laboratory, regulatory, export, quality, procurement, sales, maintenance, and administration, paired with an inventory of existing ERP/MES/SCADA and process-control automation.
a.rationale: The broad dairy occupation mix is dominated by physical roles: BLS reports 25,520 food batchmakers, 23,690 packaging/filling operators, 8,170 material movers, and 7,840 production supervisors. AI can reduce information work surrounding the process, but software alone cannot replace most plant-floor activity.

d5.bridge: USDA NASS production and USDEC export statistics measure recent national output and trade categories rather than constant-quality demand for the current service basket of eligible LMM firms. NASS reports 2025 human nonfat dry milk production of 1.66 billion pounds, up slightly, while skim milk powder fell 15.9%; USDEC reports high-protein whey exports up 6% and NFDM/SMP exports down 9% in 2025. The five-year range treats these mixed category signals as roughly stable base demand with optionality in proteins and downside in commoditized powders. It may overstate lens demand if new capacity and large integrated processors capture growth, and understate it if specialty ingredient outsourcing expands.
caveats: Production and exports are affected by milk-component availability, capacity, inventory, and trade conditions as well as end demand.
The NAICS basket spans products with materially different trajectories.
National category growth may accrue mainly to large integrated plants.
d5.change_evidence: USDA NASS five- to ten-year production, shipment, and stock series by product; USDEC and Census trade data; customer forecasts by end use; announced dryer and filtration capacity; and constant-price LMM shipment cohorts.
d5.rationale: Dry and concentrated dairy products serve durable food, beverage, nutrition, and export uses, but product mix is shifting: specialized protein categories can grow while commodity powders stagnate or decline. A near-flat base with a wide range better reflects the heterogeneous demand evidence than a single recent category trend.

caveats: The frozen LMM firm count is used as provided and not re-estimated.
Industry definitions and survey universes do not identify which enterprises are standalone, recurring external suppliers.
e.change_evidence: A complete LMM 311514 firm census matched to ownership, plant integration, captive/cooperative status, external sales, customer concentration, export exposure, normalized EBITDA, standalone systems, and transferability of certifications and customer approvals.
e.rationale: The industry supplies recurring ingredients and packaged products, but many facilities may be captive outlets for cooperative milk, integrated with cheese or fluid plants, controlled by large strategic parents, or dependent on a narrow customer or export channel. Eligible firms require standalone external-customer relationships and transferable permits, specifications, equipment, and operating capabilities.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 311514 ($1,824,217,148) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 311514 ($21,157,443,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1525 (margins.json[31]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Food Processing EBITDA/Sales 15.25% (dominant subsector of NAICS 31)); revenue/employee $1,185,756 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 311514); class <5: 24 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,089,208 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $166,104 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 5 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,415,800 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $978,410 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 12 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,672,500 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,627,556 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 28 firms, avg revenue/firm $52,356,821 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $7,984,415 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 21 firms, avg revenue/firm $204,676,238 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $31,213,126 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 32 firms, avg revenue/firm $475,217,531 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $72,470,674 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 40

caveats: The estimate concerns accountable-operator necessity, not labor intensity.
The required operator can migrate to a large integrated or overseas producer even if software cannot eliminate the role.
o.change_evidence: Customer make-versus-buy plans, announced vertical-integration and drying-capacity projects, import penetration, contract-manufacturing share, substitute-protein adoption, and regulatory changes concerning automated release or remote supervision.
o.rationale: Drying, condensing, evaporation, formulation, testing, packaging, and shipment of physical food ingredients require an accountable manufacturer with sanitary equipment, validated controls, product release, and traceability. Software may reduce information labor but cannot supply the current basket entirely; only substitution, imports, customer self-manufacture, or vertical integration reduces the lens-operator share.

caveats: No source directly measures retained AI benefit over five years.
Commodity nonfat dry milk and skim milk powder should retain less than specialized high-protein or customer-qualified ingredients.
Export exposure introduces currency, tariff, freight, and global-supply effects not included in implementation difficulty.
q.change_evidence: Customer and export contracts, commodity-index clauses, open-book costing, rebate and productivity-sharing provisions, bid histories, customer concentration, product-level margins, and pricing outcomes after prior yield or labor improvements.
q.rationale: Product pricing permits initial retention of efficiency benefits, especially in specialized proteins, formulations, or qualification-intensive products. Retention is limited by commodity reference prices, formula contracts, open-book customers, global tender competition, export volatility, private-label rebids, and rapid diffusion of common planning and document tools.

caveats: Regulatory sources establish control requirements and feasibility, not observed AI adoption.
Modern high-throughput dryers may implement faster than small plants with legacy controls.
rho.change_evidence: Plant-level architecture maps, validated AI pilots, data-quality audits, deviation and release logs, customer audit findings, cybersecurity controls, capex roadmaps, and interviews with QA, regulatory, export, and operations leaders.
rho.rationale: Planning, forecast, document, specification, certificate-of-analysis, maintenance-support, and quality-triage workflows are implementable in five years. Legacy controls, safety validation, export requirements, customer-specific formulations, cybersecurity, model reliability, and limited integration staff constrain conversion. FDA recognizes computerized records and controls but expects equivalence to regulatory intent and review of safety-critical functions.

s5.bridge: The Census Annual Business Survey infographic reports 51% of responding owners of U.S. employer businesses were age 55 or older in 2018, rather than observing control transfers for dry-dairy manufacturers. The range converts only a fraction of age-related succession exposure into a qualifying five-year control transfer after allowing for continued ownership, cooperative and corporate structures, family succession, recapitalizations, and nontransferable assets. It may overstate transfer supply if capital intensity favors institutional ownership and understate it where nutrition-ingredient consolidation generates strategic exits.
caveats: The source is cross-industry and based on 2018 owner data.
Owner age is not a transaction measure and the source counts responding owners rather than firms.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, and dairy-industry transaction records; ownership and succession mapping for the eligible firm list; and interviews with dairy lenders, ingredient-company advisers, and plant brokers.
s5.rationale: Broad owner aging creates some succession supply, but the sector's capital intensity, cooperative linkages, strategic ownership, and customer-approval requirements reduce the probability that an otherwise eligible operator actually changes control within five years.

principal_driver: Essential food and nutrition ingredient uses preserve demand for accountable physical processing.
principal_weakness: Commodity exposure, mixed product trajectories, and integrated ownership limit both benefit retention and transferable-firm availability.
